claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 13 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 13 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin. To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your child.

**First question: How old is your child?**

This is important because:
- Children **under 16** automatically qualify (subject to other conditions)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **20 or over** are not eligible for Child Benefit

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children you'd like to check eligibility for, please let me know about each of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 13 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! Let me check the eligibility for your son Alex.

**Alex is 13 years old**, which means he's under 16, so he falls into the automatic qualification age category. Now I need to check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now the next question:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

Since Alex is 13, I would expect he's not working full-time, but I need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment. This requirement ensures that children in full-time work (24+ hours per week) outside of approved education are not eligible for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all. He's still in school, so he's not doing any paid work.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since Alex is not working, let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in his own right?**

Specifically, I'm asking whether Alex receives Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit in his own name. Since he's 13 and in school, I expect the answer is that he does NOT receive these benefits, but I need to confirm this.

Does Alex receive any benefits like ESA or Universal Credit in his own name?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. He's just a regular schoolkid - no benefits at all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Next question:

**Is Alex in an apprenticeship?**

Since he's 13 and in regular school, I expect he's not in an apprenticeship. But I need to confirm because certain types of apprenticeships can affect Child Benefit eligibility:

- In England: All apprenticeships disqualify from Child Benefit
- In Scotland: Modern Apprenticeships disqualify
- In Wales: Most apprenticeships disqualify, except Foundation Apprenticeships which are approved

Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship program?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in an apprenticeship. He's only 13, so he's just doing regular school - nothing like that yet.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since Alex is not in an apprenticeship, let me move forward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check your residency status.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if **ANY** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK** (this is the most common situation)
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and meet certain conditions (working, studying, job seeking, or have sufficient resources)
4. You're a Crown servant living abroad or living in certain qualifying countries

For most people, the first option applies. **Do you normally live in the UK?** If so, you meet this requirement.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm based in Manchester, so that should be fine.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirement. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to check if you're responsible for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You meet this requirement if **ANY** of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex** (which you mentioned he lives at home with you)
2. You contribute to his upkeep by paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit would pay (through money, food, clothes, housing, etc.)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Since you mentioned Alex lives at home with you, you clearly meet the first requirement. Is that correct - Alex lives with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. Alex lives at home with me full-time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Since Alex lives with you, you meet the responsibility requirement. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. This is important to check because:

- If no one else is claiming, we can proceed
- If someone else is claiming but you both agree that you should claim instead, that's fine (they would need to stop their claim)
- If there's a dispute about who should claim, HMRC would need to decide

Is anyone else (such as Alex's other parent or another family member) currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. It's just me looking after him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check Alex's living situation to see if any special rules apply.

**Which of the following best describes Alex's current living situation?**

1. **Living normally at home with you** (which sounds like your situation)
2. In hospital for an extended period
3. In local authority care
4. Being fostered

From what you've told me, it sounds like Alex is living normally at home with you. Is that correct, or is he in any of these other situations (hospital, care, or fostering)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - he's just living normally at home with me. Nothing unusual like hospital or care or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Just one final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the local council paying for Alex's living costs or care? Since Alex lives at home with you and you're looking after him, I expect the answer is that the council is NOT paying, but I need to confirm this.

Does the local council pay towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance costs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council doesn't pay anything towards Alex. I'm covering everything myself - he just lives with me as a normal family setup.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Let me proceed to the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
# 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit

**Congratulations!** Based on the information you've provided, you meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for your son Alex.

## Why You're Eligible:

✅ **Alex is 13 years old** - under 16, so automatically qualifies  
✅ **Not working full-time** - he's in regular school  
✅ **Not receiving benefits** - no ESA or Universal Credit in his own name  
✅ **Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship** - in regular education  
✅ **You meet residency requirements** - you normally live in the UK (Manchester)  
✅ **You're responsible for Alex** - he lives with you full-time  
✅ **No other claimant** - nobody else is claiming for Alex  
✅ **Normal living situation** - living at home, not in hospital or care  
✅ **Council not paying** - you're covering all his costs yourself

## Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge** - if you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay some or all of the benefit back through tax
5. **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

You can apply here: **https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim**

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or the application process?

reasonings: Alex is eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) He is 13 years old, which is under 16 and therefore in the automatic qualification age category. (2) He is not working (and certainly not working 24+ hours per week). (3) He does not receive any benefits in his own right such as ESA or Universal Credit. (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship - he's in regular school. (5) The claimant meets UK residency requirements by normally living in the UK (Manchester). (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as they live together full-time. (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (8) Alex is living normally at home, not in hospital, care, or fostering. (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria have been met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (aged 13), who meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit. The claimant is responsible for Alex, lives in the UK, and no disqualifying factors apply. The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

(2) He is not working (and certainly not working 24+ hours per week). (3) He does not receive any benefits in his own right such as ESA or Universal Credit. (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship - he's in regular school. (5) The claimant meets UK residency requirements by normally living in the UK (Manchester). (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex as they live together full-time. (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (8) Alex is living normally at home, not in hospital, care, or fostering. (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria have been met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (aged 13), who meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit. The claimant is responsible for Alex, lives in the UK, and no disqualifying factors apply. The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
